coredata.dc:title: Respective construction of Type-II and direct Z-scheme heterostructure by selectively depositing CdS on {001} and {101} facets of TiO2 nanosheet with CDots modification: A comprehensive comparison
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jun
Zhou, Dandan
Dong, Shuangshi
Ren, Nanqi
coredata.dc:description: Directional deposition has always been a focus issue in the construction of specific heterostructure. Herein, for the first time, we have demonstrated that the CdS could be selectively deposited on {001} or {101} facets of TiO2 nanosheet, and two different charge transfer processes were formed. First, the selective deposition of CdS on {001} facets of TiO2 nanosheet ({001}TiO2/CdS) would form the Type-II heterostructure, which seriously weakened the redox ability of {001}TiO2/CdS and directly resulted in the low photocatalytic performance (4-Chlorophenol (4-CP), 61.92% in 40 min) and serious photocorrosion of CdS. In contrary, the selective deposition of CdS on {101} facets of TiO2 nanosheet ({101}TiO2/CdS) could construct direct Z-scheme heterostructure with significantly increased photocatalytic 4-CP degradation efficiency (96.12%), much higher than pristine TiO2 nanosheet (87.21%). The hybrids were further modified by carbon nanodots (CDots) ({101}TiO2/CdS/CDots) to enhance photocatalytic performance (99.84%). The obtained direct Z-scheme {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots showed excellent stability and anti-photocorrosion ability. The synergistic effect between TiO2 nanosheet, CdS and CDots was expounded through characterization analyses, and the photocatalytic reaction mechanism was proposed in detail. Toxicity assessment authenticated good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity of {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots. Our discovery was expected to drive great advances in the use of TiO2 nanosheet for environmental remediation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2 nanosheet
Selective CdS deposition
Z-scheme heterostructure
Anti-photocorrosion
Toxicity

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (A and B) and HRTEM (C − F) images of as-prepared {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots and {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots samples.
XRD patterns (A), Raman (B) and FT-IR (C) spectra of as-prepared catalysts.
UV–vis DRS spectra (A), PL spectra (B), transient photocurrent response (C) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (D) of as-prepared catalysts.
Photocatalytic degradation (A), fractional release of Cl− ions (B) and DOC removal.

Quenching experiment on ROSs during the photocatalytic degradation of 4-CP over F-TiO2 nanosheet (A), TiO2 nanosheet (B), {001}TiO2/CdS (C), {101}TiO2/CdS (D), {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots (E) and {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots (F).
Schematic photocatalytic mechanism for {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots (A) and {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots (B) nanocomposites.

Recycling runs of photocatalytic activity for as-prepared catalysts (A and B). Cumulative concentrations of released SO4 2− (C) oxidized from S2− and Cd2+ (D). UV–vis DRS evolution (E and F) of {101}TiO2/CdS, {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots, {001}TiO2/CdS and {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots before and after seven cyclic uses.

head.ce:title: Respective construction of Type-II and direct Z-scheme heterostructure by selectively depositing CdS on {001} and {101} facets of TiO2 nanosheet with CDots modification: A comprehensive comparison

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources and Environment, Ministry of Education, Jilin University, Changchun, 130021, Jilin, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130117, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, 150090, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Directional deposition has always been a focus issue in the construction of specific heterostructure. Herein, for the first time, we have demonstrated that the CdS could be selectively deposited on {001} or {101} facets of TiO2 nanosheet, and two different charge transfer processes were formed. First, the selective deposition of CdS on {001} facets of TiO2 nanosheet ({001}TiO2/CdS) would form the Type-II heterostructure, which seriously weakened the redox ability of {001}TiO2/CdS and directly resulted in the low photocatalytic performance (4-Chlorophenol (4-CP), 61.92% in 40 min) and serious photocorrosion of CdS. In contrary, the selective deposition of CdS on {101} facets of TiO2 nanosheet ({101}TiO2/CdS) could construct direct Z-scheme heterostructure with significantly increased photocatalytic 4-CP degradation efficiency (96.12%), much higher than pristine TiO2 nanosheet (87.21%). The hybrids were further modified by carbon nanodots (CDots) ({101}TiO2/CdS/CDots) to enhance photocatalytic performance (99.84%). The obtained direct Z-scheme {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots showed excellent stability and anti-photocorrosion ability. The synergistic effect between TiO2 nanosheet, CdS and CDots was expounded through characterization analyses, and the photocatalytic reaction mechanism was proposed in detail. Toxicity assessment authenticated good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity of {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots. Our discovery was expected to drive great advances in the use of TiO2 nanosheet for environmental remediation.

ce:section.ce:para: TiO2, as a typical photocatalyst, has been widely used in wastewater treatment due to its low cost and high efficiency [1–3]. It was reported that TiO2 nanosheet has better performance than TiO2 nanoparticles [4,5]. However, the further practical application TiO2 nanosheet was always hampered by the poor reactivity [2,6]. To solve this problem, numerous methods have been explored [7]. Yang et al. and Wen et al. [8,9] successfully synthesized the anatase TiO2 single crystal microsheets with exposure of high energetic {001} facet. Compared to the low-energy {101} facet (0.44 J/m2), the high-energy {001} facet (0.90 J/m2) has unique geometrical and electronic structure, and thus chemical stability, adsorptive property and catalytic reactivity [4]. Afterward, Yu et al. [10] accurately synthesized anatase TiO2 nanosheet with co-exposed {001} and {101} facets and proposed a novel “surface heterojunction” concept. The {001} and {101} facets showed different band structures and band edge positions, which prompted the transfer of photogenerated electrons and holes to {101} and {001} facets, respectively. However, several subsequent studies showed that the anatase TiO2 nanosheet with co-exposed {001} and {101} facets did not always exhibit higher activities in photocatalytic wastewater treatment because of the rapid electron/hole recombination and low solar light utilization efficiency [10–12].

The engineered heterojunctions in photocatalysts can enhance photocatalytic activity due to efficient solar light utilization and spatial separation of photogenerated electron/hole pairs [13]. Compared with other heterostructures, the combination of TiO2 and CdS (TiO2/CdS) was significant and mature [14,15]. In general, a p-n heterojunction (Type-II) would usually be formed at the contact interface of TiO2/CdS [14]. Owing to the effect of inner electric field in p-n heterojunction, the photogenerated electron/hole pairs could achieve the spatial separation, resulting in the oxidation and reduction reactions separately occurred in two different regions [14,16]. However, the Type-II charge carrier transfer process would seriously weaken the redox ability of TiO2/CdS and result in the serious photocorrosion of CdS [16,17]. Fortunately, the direct Z-scheme heterostructure could present in TiO2/CdS under some special conditions [18]. In comparison, the direct Z-scheme heterojunction could realize spatial separation of electron/hole pairs and ensure strong redox ability simultaneously, and the photocorrosion of CdS can be efficiently prevented [17,19]. Thus, it always attracted extensive concern on how to purposefully synthesize direct Z-scheme TiO2/CdS heterostructure. However, the related research has seldom been reported to date, especially the TiO2 nanosheet as a base to construct direct Z-scheme heterostructure.

It was observed that the selective deposition of CdS or noble metal particles on different crystal facets of semiconductors would form difference heterojunctions with vastly different in photocatalytic performance [11]. Thus, it was extremely meaningful to carefully control the deposition of deputy catalysts on different facets of main catalysts.

At present, the non-metallic co-catalysts have been proved to play a key role in improving both the activity and stability of photocatalysts for wastewater treatment. The CDots have been widely employed as the non-metallic co-catalyst to modify semiconductors [20]. The CDots could effectively enhance the solar light responses of CDots/semiconductor and improve photocatalytic activity as an excellent electron-transfer and electron-reservoir medium [17,21].

Herein, for the first time, we have successfully control the deposition of CdS on {001} and {101} facets of TiO2 nanosheet, respectively, and the hybrids were further modified by CDots. The obtained Type-II ternary hybrids ({001}TiO2/CdS/CDots) and direct Z-scheme ternary hybrids ({101}TiO2/CdS/CDots) were comparatively analyzed and discussed. The photocatalytic mechanisms of both hybrids were proposed and elucidated, respectively. Especially, we further comparatively investigated the photocorrosion of as-prepared photocatalysts by observing the change in content of SO4 2− (transformed from S2−) and Cd2+ in seven cyclic 4-CP degradation tests. The toxicity of the {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots nanocomposites toward S. aureus and E. coli was also evaluated. Hence, this work may be of interest to both materials scientists and those working in the areas of photocatalytic water purification.

All reagents were used directly without further treatment. The CDots was prepared via a typical electrochemical method [22]. The anatase TiO2 nanosheet was synthesized through a facile hydrothermal process according to previous literatures [11], and the obtained sample was labelled as F-TiO2 nanosheet due to the existence of F− ions on the sample surface. To remove F− ions, the F-TiO2 nanosheet was further calcined in a muffle furnace at 550 °C for 2 h, and the collected powder was accordingly denoted as TiO2 nanosheet. The calcination not only removed the fluoride ions thoroughly but formed better crystallinity. The processes of the addition of CdS and CDots on the surface of F-TiO2 nanosheet or TiO2 nanosheet were exhibited in Figure S1. The addition of CdS were conducted via a photo-deposition method [23]. In a typical procedure, 0.50 g F-TiO2 nanosheet or TiO2 nanosheet powders were respectively uniformly dispersed in ethanol (20 mL) and water (60 mL) mixed solution, followed by adding 838.78 mg Cd(ClO4)2·6H2O and 51.20 mg S8 into the above mixture with vigorous stirring for 1 h. Then, the suspension was irradiated by 150 W Xenon lamp (CEL-HXF 300, Beijing Education Au-light Co., Ltd.) for 6 h under continuous stirring. The resulted precipitation was collected, washed with ultrapure water for 3 times, and then dried at 60 °C overnight to obtain {001}TiO2/CdS and {101}TiO2/CdS nanocomposites. For comparison, the pure CdS was also prepared with above described deposition method by adding nothing. The CDots was introduced through direct mixing process. 0.10 g as-prepared {001}TiO2/CdS or {101}TiO2/CdS powders were suspended in 50 mL ultrapure water, after that 2 mL CDots solution (2 mg mL−1) was added into the suspension with vigorous stirring for 1 h. Then the precipitates were collected and washed with ultrapure water for 3 times, and finally dried at 60 °C overnight. The obtained samples were denoted as {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots or {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots.
The details on characterization methods were listed in supplementary materials.

A 150 W Xenon lamp (CEL-HXF 300, Beijing Education Au-light Co., Ltd.) was chosen as the light source, and the spectrum was shown in Figure S2. In a typical experiment, 0.10 g photocatalysts were fully dissolved in 100 mL 4-CP (20 mg L–1) aqueous solution. Prior to irradiation, the suspension was adequately stirring for 1 h in dark to reach adsorption/desorption equilibrium. In the process of irradiation, 0.5 mL suspension was sampled regularly and filtered by a 0.45 μm membrane before detection. The stability and repeatability of photocatalysts were measured by repeating the identical experiment for 7 times.

The scavenger quenching experiments were carried out in the same condition except the addtion of the scavengers. The details were sodium chromate (VI) (0.05 mM, Sigma, 99.5%) for electron, sodium oxalate (0.5 mM, Sigma, 99.5%) for hole, isopropanol (0.5 mM, Sigma, 99.5%) for hydroxyl radical (
OH) and 2,2,6,6-Tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPO) for superoxide radical (
O2–) (1 mM, Sigma, 99%). The volume ratio of scavenger and 4-CP was 1:100.
The details on analytical methods were listed in supplementary materials.

S. aureus and E. coli were chosen to investigate the cell growth inhibition of {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots nanocomposites. The specific experimental procedure was published elsewhere [17,24]. All the bacterial cultures treated for 18 h with different {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots concentrations (0, 1, 2, 5, 10, 15 and 20 mg mL−1) were plated on the Mueller−Hinton agar (MHA) and then incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. The inability of the treated cultures to form bacterial colonies was scored as bacterial death.

The TEM images of {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots (Fig. 1 A) and {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots (Fig. 1B) showed a typical nanosheet shape of anatase TiO2 with co-exposed {001} and {101} facets, and the average side length and thickness were ∼50 and ∼10 nm, respectively [10,23]. The results indicated that the addition of CdS and CDots negligibly altered the morphology of nanosheet, namely the CdS and CDots were only deposited on the surfaces of the TiO2 nanosheets rather than entering into the lattice internal of them [17,21]. The HRTEM images further depict the connections between CdS, CDots and TiO2 nanosheets. It can be seen that the CdS was selectively deposited on {001} facets of TiO2 nanosheet in {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots (Fig. 1C and E) and {101} facets of TiO2 nanosheet in {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots (Fig. 1D and F). The lattice spacing of 0.336 nm and 0.316 nm were corresponding well to the (111) and (101) planes of cubic CdS, respectively [17,25]. The introduction of CDots was non-selective (Fig. 1C–F). Numerous CDots were uniformly dispersed on the surface of {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots and {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots. The clear lattice fringes with the distance of 0.321 nm and 0.212 nm could be assigned to (100) and (101) planes of graphitic carbon, respectively [21,22]. The intimate contact between TiO2 nanosheet, CdS and CDots was presumably beneficial to the vectorial transfer of electron, thus enhancing the charge separation and photocatalytic activity [17,26]. The EDS mapping images (Figure S3) show that the {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots hybrid contained the elements Ti, O, Cd, S and C with a uniform distribution of CdS and CDots over the entire TiO2 nanosheet surface.

In Fig. 2 A, both F-TiO2 nanosheet and TiO2 nanosheet showed a well crystalline that was indexed to anatase phase of TiO2 (JCPDS, No. 21-1272) [27,28]. In comparison, the XRD diffraction peaks of TiO2 nanosheet were narrower, sharper and stronger than those of F-TiO2 nanosheet. The result indicated that the better crystallinity would form in the process of removing F− ions, which was ascribed to further high temperature calcination [1,29]. Complete crystallization was conducive to improving the photocatalytic performance [5,29]. The peaks at 26.6°, 44°, and 52° in XRD pattern of CdS were corresponding to the (111), (220), and (311) plane diffraction of the cubic phase of CdS (JCPDS, No. 89-0440), respectively [25]. No changes appeared in the XRD diffraction peaks of anatase TiO2 after the addition of CdS and CDots. This meant that the CdS and CDots were only deposited on the surfaces of F-TiO2 nanosheet or TiO2 nanosheet, rather than entering into the lattice internal and influencing the growth of TiO2 [17,21]. The presence of CdS and CDots wolud slightly decrease the peak intensities due to their shield effect, further confirming that the CdS and CDots were successfully deposited on the surfaces of TiO2 [17,21]. Low contents and fine particle sizes of CdS and CDots resulted in the absence of their characteristic diffraction peaks [17,21].

The peaks at around 300 and 600 cm−1 in Raman spectrum of CdS (in Fig. 2B) were ascribed to first-order longitudinal optic phonons (1LO) and second-order phonons (2LO), respectively [17,30]. Raman spectra of F-TiO2 nanosheet and TiO2 nanosheet were similar in appearance. As shown in Fig. 2B, four strong peaks at 139, 395, 515 and 642 cm−1 were corresponding to the Eg(1), B1g(1), A1g + B1g(2) and Eg(3) modes of anatase TiO2, respectively [31]. After the CdS deposition, the characteristic peak intensity of TiO2 was weaken obviously, and the characteristic peak intensity of CdS was enhanced relatively. This phenomenon resulted from the strong interaction between CdS and TiO2 [17,18]. In spectra of {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots and {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots, two additional peaks at 1331 and 1604 cm−1 were attributed to the D-band and the G-band of carbon, respectively [21,31]. The results demonstrated the successful combination of CDots, CdS and TiO2 nanosheet [17].

In the FT-IR spectra of F-TiO2 nanosheet and TiO2 nanosheet (Fig. 2C), peaks at 3550 − 2950 and 1640 cm−1were attributed to OH stretching vibrations of surface adsorbed H2O [1,17]. For CdS, two peaks at 1381 and 1120 cm−1 were corresponding well to the vibrations of SCd bonds [14,17]. After CdS doping, the characteristic peaks of CdS could be detected in the spectra of {001}TiO2/CdS, {101}TiO2/CdS, {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots and {101}TiO2/CdS/CDot. Besides, both {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots and {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots showed obvious absorption at 1640 and 1441 cm−1, which were ascribed to the OC and OC stretching vibrations, respectively [21,31]. This confirmed the successful addition of CDots [17,21]. It was worth noting that the characteristic peaks position of CdS and CDots possessed the relatively slight red-shift after deposited on TiO2, which was caused by the strong interaction between them [17,21]. The results were in a good agreement with above XRD and Raman analyses.

The survey spectrum (Figure S3) presented the existence of Ti, O, Cd, S, F and C in {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots and Ti, O, Cd, S and C in {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots. As shown in Fig. 2D, both {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots and {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots showed two distinct peaks at 458.6 and 464.1 eV, corresponding to the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 of anatase TiO2, respectively [1,31]. In Fig. 2E, two fitted peaks at 529.7 and 531.9 eV in {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots and 529.9 and 531.8 eV in {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots were ascribed to OTi and OC bonds, respectively [31]. In Cd 3d spectra (Fig. 2F) of {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots and {101}TiO2/CdS/CDot, the peaks at 412.1 and 405.2 eV belonged to Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2 of Cd2+, respectively [30]. The S 2p spectra (Fig. 2G) were resolved into two peaks located at 161.0 and 162.1 eV in {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots and 161.1 and 162.2 eV in {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots, which were assigned to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively [25,30]. For {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots, the F 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2H) showed one obvious peak at around 684.1 eV, which was identified as the characteristic peak of F− ions [32]. In comparison, there was no F 1 s signal in spectrum of {101}TiO2/CdS/CDot (Fig. 2H), indicating the F− ions were completely removed after further high temperature calcination [10,11]. A broad peak appeared in C 1 s spectra (Fig. 2I) of {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots and {101}TiO2/CdS/CDot, suggesting the successful introduction of CDots [17,21]. XPS analyses results further confirmed the presence of CdS and CDots and revealed their strong interaction with TiO2.

In Fig. 3 A, both TiO2 nanosheet and F-TiO2 nanosheet showed no absorption in the region of 395–800 nm, which accorded with the intrinsic absorption property of TiO2 [11]. The pristine CdS exhibited strong adsorption with wavelengths shorter than 600 nm, in agreement with previous literatures [25]. After the deposition of CdS, the {101}TiO2/CdS and {001}TiO2/CdS showed enhanced visible light absorption with an obvious red shift of the absorption edge (< 550 nm), probably owing to interfacial interaction between TiO2 and CdS [18,23]. In comparison, the introduction of CDots could further improved the visible light absorption of {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots and {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots, especially in the region of 550–800 nm, resulting from the superimposed enhancement of visible light absorption of each component. The intense and wide absorption across the visible light region would generate more photogenerated electron/hole pairs and promote a higher photocatalytic activity [21]. The changing color of photocatalysts could further confirm the successful addition of CdS and CDots (Figure S4).

As shown in Fig. 3B, two obvious strong emission peaks at ∼361 and ∼393 nm belonged to the fast recombination of photogenerated electron/hole pairs in TiO2 [33]. In comparison, the PL intensities significantly decreased after the deposition of CdS, illustrating a slow recombination of photogenerated electron/hole pairs in the {101}TiO2/CdS and {001}TiO2/CdS [17,33]. In particular, the {101}TiO2/CdS showed a lower PL intensity than {001}TiO2/CdS, demonstrating that the CdS selectively depositing on {101} facets of TiO2 nanosheet was more conductive to prevent the recombination of photogenerated electron/hole pairs and to further enhance the photocatalytic performance. Moreover, the addition of CDots could further effectively facilitate the transfer and inhibit the recombination of the photogenerated electron/hole pairs in {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots and {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots.

In Fig. 3C, both TiO2 nanosheet and F-TiO2 nanosheet showed low photocurrent response in that the TiO2 could only absorb UV light (< 395 nm, in Fig. 3A) and be excited to generate electron/hole pairs [31]. After the deposition of CdS, the {101}TiO2/CdS and {001}TiO2/CdS all exhibited the fast, steady and reproducible photocurrent responses during several consecutive on − off irradiation cycles. The introduction of CDots could further significantly enhance the photocurrent responses. The {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots possessed the highest transfer and separation rate of photogenerated electron/hole pairs, ∼1.3 times higher than that of {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots. This illustrated that the CdS selectively depositing on {101} facets of TiO2 nanosheet could generate more available photogenerated electrons and increase photocurrent responses. As depicted in Fig. 3D, the {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots possessed the smallest arc radius of the EIS Nyquist plot, suggesting the highest efficiency of separation and transport of photogenerated charge carriers [24]. The result was in agreement with the DRS, PL and transient photocurrent response analyses.

No appreciable change of 4-CP concentration in the negative control (no catalyst) demonstrated the photochemically stable of 4-CP [17,34]. All photocatalysts possessed almost identical removal efficiency of 4-CP by adsorption, indicating their similar adsorption capacities. As observed, the 4-CP removal efficiency in 40 min decreased in the order {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots (99.84%) > {101}TiO2/CdS (96.12%) > TiO2 nanosheet (87.21%) > F-TiO2 nanosheet (71.77%) > {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots (67.53%) > {001}TiO2/CdS (61.92%). The much higher photocatalytic activity of TiO2 nanosheet than F-TiO2 nanosheet was caused by the better crystallinity and higher number of electrons in TiO2 nanosheet (see in Figs. 2 and 3) [11,35]. In addition, the fluoride ions existed on the surface of TiO2 were probably occupying active sites which were crucial for photocatalytic reaction. The introduction of CdS significantly improved the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 nanosheet, but the 4-CP removal efficiency decreased dramatically after depositing CdS on F-TiO2 nanosheet. Both {101}TiO2/CdS and {001}TiO2/CdS showed stronger light response and more efficient charge transfer capability than TiO2 nanosheet and F-TiO2 nanosheet correspondingly (Fig. 3). Thus, the difference of photocatalytic activity were most likely caused by the specific heterostructure existed in {101}TiO2/CdS and {001}TiO2/CdS, respectively. These results indicated that the deposition of CdS on {101} facets of TiO2 nanosheet was superior to that on {001} facets of F-TiO2 nanosheet in terms of photocatalytic performance. Besides, the addition of CDots could equally improve the 4-CP degradation efficiency of {101}TiO2/CdS and {001}TiO2/CdS, which was attributed to the synergistic effect between CdS, CDots and TiO2 [17,24,26]. The degradation kinetics were conducted to quantitatively understand the degradation of 4-CP and to further compare the photocatalytic activities of as-prepared catalysts. As shown in Figure S5, the {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots possessed the highest apparent reaction rate constant (k value) over the samples, indicating its highest photocatalytic activity under the same conditions. As above, the calculated k values showed similar trends relative to 4-CP removal efficiency. Fig. 4 B presented the dechlorination in the entire photocatalytic 4-CP degradation process. As observed, the {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots exhibited the highest released ratio of Cl− of 99.76% in 40 min. This suggested that the photocatalytic products were almost fully dechlorinated, which was conductive to further degradation. Simultaneously, the mineralization efficiency of 4-CP was 94.28%, which was much higher than other samples under the identical conditions (Fig. 4C). Efficiency of 4-CP (C).

As shown in Fig. 5 A and B, electrons (e−), superoxide radicals (
O2 −), hydroxyl radicals (
OH) and holes (h+) were all present in F-TiO2 nanosheet and TiO2 nanosheet without exception, which was consistent with existing literatures [27,36]. Both e− and h+ were main ROSs in F-TiO2 nanosheet and TiO2 nanosheet, while
OH and
O2 − were less ones. This indicated that only a small amount of e− and h+ could be captured by O2 and H2O, respectively and converted into
O2 − and
OH on the surface of F-TiO2 nanosheet and TiO2 nanosheet. After the CdS deposition (Fig. 5C and D), the e− and h+ were also main ROSs in {001}TiO2/CdS and {101}TiO2/CdS. The content of
O2 − increased relatively. The difference was that the
OH was slightly improved in {101}TiO2/CdS in concentration but almost disappeared in {001}TiO2/CdS. More
O2 − and
OH were conductive to enhance the photocatalytic activity [17], which was in agreement with the results of photocatalytic 4-CP degradation. In Fig. 5E and F, the addition of CDots could substantially increase the amounts of
O2 −, and the content of e− was sharply reduced in {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots and {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots. This showed that the CDots could effectively facilitate the electron transfer and the generation of
O2 − from e−, thus further improving the photocatalytic degradation rate. In addition, the concentration of
OH also significantly increased in {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots, indicating that the introduction of CDots could promote the combination of h+ and adsorbed H2O to form
OH. Almost no
OH existed in {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots, demonstrating that it was still unable to generate these ROSs after CDots modification, while only provided more available h+ in comparison.

To generate
O2 − and
OH on the surface of the photocatalyst, the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) positions should be more negative and positive than −0.046 eV (vs. NHE, the standard redox potential of O2/
O2 −) and 2.30 eV (vs. NHE, the standard redox potential of OH−/
OH), respectively [21,37]. According to previous reports [33], the CB and VB of TiO2 were positioned at around −0.21 and 2.90 eV, respectively, which met the condition for
O2 − and
OH generation. Similarly, the CB and VB positions of CdS were about −0.60 and 1.70 eV, respectively [17,25]. Thus, the e− located in the CB of the CdS could combine with adsorbed O2 to generate
O2 −, but the h+ existed in the VB of CdS was thermodynamically unable to react with adsorbed H2O to produce
OH. The ESR spectroscopy was in good agreement with the results of scavenger quenching experiments (See Figure S6).

Based on above results and literatures [11,17,26,30], the charge transfer and photocatalytic mechanisms of {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots and {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots were proposed in Fig. 6 . A typical Type-II and a direct Z-scheme heterostructures had formed in {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots and {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots, respectively. Under light irradiation, both TiO2 and CdS were excited to generate e−/h+ pairs. In the internal of TiO2, the photogenerated e− and h+ would spontaneously migrated and gathered on {101} and {001} facets, respectively, due to the presence of crystal surface heterojunction [11,38]. In Fig. 6A, when the p-type CdS was selectively deposited on the h+-rich {001} facets of n-type TiO2, the p-n heterojunction would be formed at the contact interface of CdS and F-TiO2 nanosheet. Under the effect of inner electric field in p-n heterojunction, the e− in the CB of CdS and h+ in the VB of F-TiO2 nanosheet could effectively transfer to the CB of F-TiO2 nanosheet and VB of CdS, respectively. In theory [39], the recombination of e−/h+ pairs could be effectively inhibited via spatial separation, extending the lifetime of the charge carriers (in Fig. 3). The accumulated e− on {101} facets could further transfer to CDots through the contact interface due to their excellent electronic conductivity, which likely favored the generation of
O2 − [38]. The enriched h+ in the VB of CdS would migrate to the surface of CDots and directly decompose the surface-adsorbed organics. In this way, the e−/h+ pairs achieved spatial separation, and the oxidation and reduction processes separately occurred in different regions. However, the Type-II charge carrier transfer process seriously weakened the redox ability of {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots and directly resulted in the low photocatalytic performance (Figs. 4 and 5) [16]. In contrast, when the CdS was selectively deposited on the e−-rich {101} facets of TiO2, a direct Z-scheme heterostructure would form between TiO2 nanosheet and CdS (Fig. 6B). Two main differences existed in the {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots. (1) The e− in the CB of TiO2 nanosheet with low reduction activity could directly transfer to CdS and recombine with the h+ in the VB of CdS with low oxidation activity, retaining a greater amount of the high-energy e− and h+ on the CdS and TiO2 nanosheet, respectively. (2) Similarly, the direct Z-scheme heterojunction also achieved the spatial separation of e−/h+ pairs, as well as the oxidation and reduction reactions separately occurred in two different regions [19]. The recombination of photogenerated e−/h+ pairs was significantly inhibited. It is the most important that the Z-scheme charge transfer process would further enhance the redox ability of {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots, which contributed to the enhanced photocatalytic performance (Figs. 4 and 5) [19]. More e− in CdS and h+ in TiO2 nanosheet were available to generate
O2 − and
OH, then to drive the photocatalytic redox processes. In a word, the formation of a Z-scheme heterostructure not only effectively extended the lifetime of the photogenerated charge carriers (see in Fig. 3) but promoted the redox ability of the system at the same time. Only through above charge carrier transfer and photocatalytic mechanisms, the experimental findings could be explained reasonably.

As depicted in Fig. 7 A, the TiO2 nanosheet, {101}TiO2/CdS and {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots all showed excellent stability and reusability with negligible change, at ∼1.57%, ∼2.11% and ∼1.82% differences, respectively, for 4-CP photocatalytic degradation after seven cycles. The as-prepared F-TiO2 nanosheet also exhibited high stability and reusability with only slight decreases of the 4-CP degradation efficiency (∼1.46%) after seven cycles (Fig. 7B). However, after the deposition of CdS, the activity of the obtained {001}TiO2/CdS and {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots significantly enhanced in second cycle and continuously increased in the next five cycles, and the final 4-CP removal efficiency were relatively improving ∼2.73% and ∼11.29%, respectively under identical conditions. The results were caused by the photo-corrosion of CdS. The TiO2 nanosheet and F-TiO2 nanosheet showed well stability and reusability due to their physically and chemically stable. After the deposition of CdS, a direct Z-scheme and typical Type-II heterostructures had formed in {101}TiO2/CdS and {001}TiO2/CdS, respectively. In {101}TiO2/CdS system, the e− in the CB of TiO2 nanosheet would directly transfer to CdS and recombine with the h+ in the VB of CdS, completely consumed the h+ in CdS and fundamentally inhibited the photo-corrosion process. In contrast, a serious photocorrosion existed in {001}TiO2/CdS. The Type-II charge carrier transfer process seriously weakened the redox ability of {001}TiO2/CdS and directly led to the low photocatalytic performance. Thus, the 4-CP removal efficiency of {001}TiO2/CdS increased continuously with the detachment of CdS via photocorrosion. After two cycles, most of the CdS was greatly photo-corroded and the content decreased significantly. The addition of CDots nearly would not improve the anti-photocorrosion ability of {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots, but effectively enhanced its photocatalytic performance because of the efficient electron transfer [17].

The content of S and Cd in the treated solutions was detected in seven cyclic 4-CP degradation tests. According to materials preparation methods, in the as-prepared photocatalysts systems, the initial mass of S and Cd were all equal, respectively. The cumulatively released SO4 2− (transformed from S2−) reached 257.31 and 248.67 mg L−1 in the {001}TiO2/CdS and {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots systems, respectively, in the seven cycles. The release percentages of SO4 2− were 95.30% and 92.10% when compared with the initial concentration, respectively (Fig. 7C). In contrast, only 0.63% (1.70 mg L−1) and 0.41% (1.11 mg L−1) SO4 2− finally released in the {101}TiO2/CdS and {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots systems, respectively. Similarly, the cumulatively released Cd2+ contents reached 298.74 and 292.35 mg L−1 and the release percentages were 98.32% and 96.23% (Fig. 7D) in the {001}TiO2/CdS and {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots systems, respectively. The cumulatively released Cd2+ percentages in the {101}TiO2/CdS and {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots systems were only 0.64% (1.92 mg L−1) and 0.42% (1.25 mg L−1), respectively. Both SO4 2− and Cd2+ were suddenly and significantly released in the second cycle using the {001}TiO2/CdS and {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots systems, further confirmed the results of the above recycling reactions. These results further confirmed the superiority of the Z-scheme {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots for photocatalytic chemical wastewater treatment over the Type-II {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots under the identical conditions.

According to previous reports [40–42], the released SO4 2− and Cd2+ were affecting the stability of catalysts in various degree. Especially, the Cd2+ ions were very likely to adsorb directly on the titania-water interface and act as bridges between the surface and the polyelectrolyte [40]. The formation of the titania-Cd2+-polyelectrolyte complex improved the colloidal stability of the dispersions significantly.

UV–vis DRS was carried out to compare the optical absorption ability of the fresh and used as-prepared samples (Fig. 7E and F). There were nearly no obvious DRS changes in {101}TiO2/CdS and {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots before and after seven cycles, indicating their excellent photochemical stabilities as well as the anti-photocorrosion ability [17]. In comparison, an apparent DRS change can be observed between the fresh and used {001}TiO2/CdS and {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots samples. It was noted that the optical-absorption properties of F-TiO2 nanosheet appeared remarkably in {001}TiO2/CdS and {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots after seven cycles, which was attributed to the chemically oxidative decomposition of the external CdS, followed by the exposure of the F-TiO2 nanosheet substrate.

The toxicity assessment of nanomaterials was particularly important for their potential application in environmental purification. In this work, the cytotoxicity assays with S. aureus (Figure S7 A − C) and E. coli (Figure S7D − F) as models were operated to investigate the possible toxicity of as-prepared {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots. The corresponding inhibition distances were showing the toxic effects. The bigger clear zones were representing stronger toxicity [43]. As observed, there was almost no observable inhibition of either S. aureus or E. coli, even while the dosage of catalyst was as high as 20 mg mL−1. The results demonstrated the low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility of {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots.

We have demonstrated that the CdS could be selectively deposited on {101} or {001} facets of TiO2 nanosheet, and the hybrids were further modified by CDots. The direct Z-scheme charge carrier transfer process in {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots remarkably enhanced the photocatalytic activity, excellent stability and anti-photocorrosion ability. However, the Type-II charge carrier transfer process seriously weakened the redox ability of {001}TiO2/CdS/CDots and directly resulted in the low photocatalytic performance and serious photocorrosion of CdS. The co-catalysts CDots synchronously acted as an excellent electron-transfer and electron-reservoir center to further improve photocatalytic performance. Toxicity assessment authenticated good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity of {101}TiO2/CdS/CDots. We believe this discovery was expected to drive great advances in the use of TiO2 nanosheet for environmental remediation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51678270, 51761145031).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2018.12.013.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
